Projects

01

Hardware · PCB Design · Audio Engineering · CMPE 110 — Fall 2025

2-Watt Stereo Audio Amplifier

Designed, simulated, and physically manufactured a dual-channel stereo audio amplifier PCB from scratch. The project covered the complete engineering lifecycle — from circuit theory and simulation in LTSpice, through schematic capture and multi-layer PCB layout in KiCad, to through-hole soldering and oscilloscope verification. The board successfully amplified stereo audio at 2 watts per channel, verified clean output at 15kHz, 20kHz, and 25kHz on a Tektronix DPO 3032 oscilloscope.

KiCad 9.0LTSpiceLM386 Op-AmpGerber / Drill FilesThrough-hole SolderingOscilloscope TestingBOM ExportERC Verification

Step 1 — Design & Simulation (LTSpice)

Before touching KiCad, the entire circuit was modeled and verified in LTSpice. The LM386 op-amp was chosen for its low-voltage audio performance. A gain network was configured using specific capacitor values to hit the 2W target output, and a potentiometer was wired at the input to serve as a physical volume control. Waveform simulation confirmed the signal amplified cleanly with zero distortion or clipping across the full frequency range.

Fig 1 — LTSpice schematic: three LM386 op-amp stages configured for dual-channel stereo output. Left channel (top) and right channel (bottom) each run through a 47k resistor and 10k potentiometer before hitting the amplifier stage. Capacitor values were tuned specifically to hit the gain target without distortion.

Step 2 — Schematic Capture (KiCad)

With simulation confirmed, the circuit was fully recreated in KiCad's Schematic Editor. This defined exactly how every component connects — power switch, LED indicator, dual amplifier stages, speaker terminals, and both left and right channel inputs. After wiring, the Electrical Rules Check (ERC) was run to catch any mistakes before moving to layout.

ERC Result

0 errors · 0 warnings — all pin connections verified, power flags confirmed, schematic locked in before PCB layout began.

Fig 2 — Final KiCad schematic: complete stereo amplifier with SW1 power switch, D1 LED power indicator, dual LM386 amplifier stages (U1, U2, U3), screw terminal speaker outputs, and separate left/right channel input jacks. 470uF bulk caps handle power supply filtering; 0.047uF bypass caps keep each amplifier stage clean.

Step 3 — Footprint Assignment

Every schematic symbol was mapped to its physical real-world package. The LM386 was assigned Package_DIP:DIP-8_W7.62mm — the standard through-hole 8-pin DIP package. Each capacitor was physically measured in the lab and matched to the correct CP_Radial footprint diameter. Pin numbers were cross-checked against the LM386 datasheet to confirm nothing was miswired before moving to layout.

Fig 3 — KiCad footprint assignment table: all 26 components mapped from schematic symbols to physical package sizes. Left column shows library entries, center shows component-to-footprint assignments, right shows filtered matches. Three LM386 ICs assigned DIP-8_W7.62mm; capacitors split across D6mm, D5mm, and D16mm CP_Radial footprints based on physical measurements.

Step 4 — PCB Layout: Place & Route

The most technically challenging phase. All 26 components were placed onto the board and every electrical connection manually routed as copper traces across two layers. Working with two copper layers (F.Cu and B.Cu) introduced the need for vias — small plated holes that allow a trace to jump between layers — which required careful planning to avoid signal interference. The initial rat's nest of crossing connections required multiple full rearrangements of components before a clean routing solution emerged.

01

Layer management

First experience with two-layer PCB routing. Had to learn when to place vias to jump between F.Cu and B.Cu without creating accidental shorts or crossing signal paths.

02

The rat's nest

Initial component placement produced a dense web of crossing connections. The board required three full rearrangements to untangle the net topology into something routable.

03

Routing conflicts

Even after rearrangement, traces still blocked each other on the single copper layer. Required systematic trial-and-error to find routing paths that worked for all 26 nets simultaneously.

Fig 4 — Final PCB layout in KiCad: all copper traces routed across F.Cu (blue) and B.Cu (red) layers. Three LM386 DIP-8 footprints visible in the center columns, surrounded by radial capacitors and screw terminals at the board edges. No DRC violations on final submission.

Fig 5 — KiCad 3D render: component placement verified before generating manufacturing files. Large blue cylinders are 470uF electrolytic caps, center ICs are the three LM386 chips, circular outlines are potentiometers, red LED at top left is the power indicator.

Step 5 — PCB Assembly & Soldering

After the manufacturing files were sent out and the bare board returned, all 26 components were manually inserted and soldered through-hole. This phase was significantly harder than the design phases. Solder bridges formed between adjacent pins on the DIP-8 packages, and several cold joints required desoldering and rework. The repeated heating from rework made some pads fragile — but the board was ultimately fully assembled with every component in place.

Fig 6 — Physical board progression: bare PCB front showing silkscreen component labels and pad locations (left); copper back layer showing routed traces and through-hole solder points (center); fully assembled board after soldering all components — LM386 chips, 470uF caps, potentiometers, LED, resistors, and screw terminals all in place (right).

Step 6 — Testing & Verification

With the board fully assembled, a 9V power supply was connected. The power indicator LED lit immediately — confirming correct VCC and GND rail connections throughout the board. Audio input was then applied and the output captured on a Tektronix DPO 3032 Digital Phosphor Oscilloscope at three frequencies to verify clean amplification across the audible range.

Power test

LED lit immediately on 9V connection — VCC and GND rails confirmed wired correctly through the board.

Audio output

Stereo amplified audio confirmed through both speaker channels. Clean signal with no clipping observed.

Frequency range

Clean sinusoidal waveforms verified at 15kHz, 20kHz, and 25kHz on Tektronix DPO 3032.

Fig 7 — Tektronix DPO 3032 oscilloscope output: clean amplified sinusoidal waveforms at 15kHz (left), 20kHz (center), and 25kHz (right). All three frequencies show consistent amplitude with no distortion, clipping, or signal degradation — confirming the amplifier performs correctly across the full tested frequency range.

Step 7 — Manufacturing File Generation

The final step exported all files needed for professional PCB fabrication. KiCad's Plot function generated Gerber files for each physical board layer: F.Cu and B.Cu for copper traces, F.Mask and B.Mask for solder mask coating, F.SilkS for component reference labels, and Edge.Cuts for the board outline. A drill file (.drl) was generated for CNC through-hole machining. A full Bill of Materials was exported as CSV. All Gerber files were visually inspected in GerbView to confirm trace continuity and correct drill hole placement before final submission.

Fig 8 — KiCad GerbView: B.Cu copper layer (left) showing all back-side traces and drill points; B.Mask solder mask layer (right) showing exposed pad openings. Both layers verified for trace continuity and correct drill hole alignment before submission.

Fig 9 — Bill of Materials exported from KiCad as CSV: 4× 470uF caps, 3× 22uF caps, 3× 0.047uF bypass caps, 3× LM386 ICs, resistors, 2× potentiometers, 1× SW_SPDT switch, 1× LED, and 5× screw terminal connectors — complete parts list ready for assembly.

Project Outcome

Complete PCB engineering lifecycle executed from circuit theory to physical manufactured product. Confirmed stereo audio amplification at 2W per channel with clean output verified across the 15–25kHz frequency range on professional test equipment.

04

Hardware · RF Circuits · Signal Analysis · EE 97 — Fall 2024

RF Resonant Circuit & Frequency Tuning

Built and analyzed an RF resonant LC circuit on a breadboard using an SJSU EE97 coil module. The circuit was tuned across frequencies by adjusting the resonant point, with output behavior measured on a Tektronix DPO 3012 oscilloscope. Observed resonant frequency behavior at approximately 3.1–3.15 MHz with peak-to-peak voltages ranging from 524mV to 8.56V across different tuning configurations — demonstrating the characteristic voltage amplification of a resonant LC circuit operating near its natural frequency.

LC Resonant CircuitRF InductorsBreadboard PrototypingOscilloscope AnalysisFrequency Tuning3.1MHz Signal Range

Resonant frequency

Signal locked at 3.104–3.149 MHz across all tuning configurations.

Peak output

8.56V peak-to-peak at resonance — demonstrating LC voltage step-up behavior.

Input signal

524mV input confirmed on oscilloscope at 3.141 MHz baseline.

Circuit Setup

Full circuit overview: SJSU EE97 RF coil module mounted on the Jameco breadboard with red/black/yellow power wires running to the external DC supply terminals (Va, Vb, Vc). White wire carries the input signal from the function generator into the LC network. The black hat is the speaker enclosure used to confirm audio output at resonant frequency.

Close-up of the SJSU EE97 coil module: two copper-wound RF inductors visible on the green PCB alongside the ferrite core tuning component. Red and yellow oscilloscope probe clips connect to the output nodes of the LC network to capture the signal at each tuned frequency point.

Alternate board angle showing the full passive component network wired below the RF module — resistors and additional components visible on the breadboard rows, connecting the coil module output to the measurement and load circuit. Black oscilloscope ground cable routes off-board to the Tektronix.

Circuit live and operating: speaker enclosure connected to the output to provide audio confirmation of signal at resonant frequency. Blue LED glow visible at the base of the speaker — indicating active signal flow through the circuit. This confirmed the LC circuit was passing and amplifying the signal at the tuned resonant point.

04

Full Stack · Healthcare · Live Product · Independently Built

Sugar Scout

Sugar Scout is a full-stack diabetic health platform designed, built, and deployed independently. It goes far beyond a simple informational website — it's a multi-tool application with 9 distinct sections including an interactive glucose curve simulator, a real-time insulin decay calculator, a sauce and condiment sugar database, a restaurant guide, a free supply finder for uninsured diabetics, a community events directory, and more. Built to give diabetics practical, real-world tools they can actually use day-to-day. Live at sugarscout.vercel.app.

JavaScriptHTML / CSSVercel9 Feature SectionsInteractive CalculatorsReal-time UIHealthcare
Visit sugarscout.vercel.app ↗

Homepage & Restaurant Guide

Sugar Scout homepage: full navigation with 9 sections — Restaurants, Nutrition, Sauce Scanner, The Lab, IOB Math, Supplies, Hacks, Events, and Tips. The Restaurant Guide section shown features a live search bar and category filters (Sweets & Bakeries, Casual Dining, Fast Food, Coffee Tea & Juice) for navigating diabetic-friendly eating options.

Restaurant Watchouts page: per-chain warnings and better-choice recommendations for popular chains including Panda Express, McDonald's, and Burger King. Each entry includes a specific WARNING (what to avoid and why) and a BETTER CHOICE (exact menu items that are safer). Built to give diabetics actionable guidance rather than vague dietary advice.

Sauce Scanner — Interactive Sugar Database

Sauce Scanner in interactive mode: tap any condiment (BBQ, Teriyaki, Ketchup, Buffalo, Ranch, Mayo, Honey Mustard) to instantly analyze its sugar content. The circular Sugar Meter updates in real time — ketchup shown here at 4g, rated Moderate Sugar on the green/yellow/red scale. Built as a tool diabetics can use mid-meal to make quick decisions.

Sauce Scanner database view: every entry rated Deadly / Danger / Caution / Safe with exact sugar and carb counts per serving. Applebee's Honey BBQ shown at 22g sugar / 24g carbs, rated Deadly. Blaze Pizza Classic Red at 0g sugar, rated Safe. The database covers condiments across dozens of restaurant chains — a resource that doesn't exist anywhere else in this format.

The Lab — Glucose Curve Simulator

The Lab — full glucose curve simulation engine: estimated blood glucose output (178 mg/dL) plotted as an orange curve against the target zone over a 4-hour window. Total net change shown as +90. Input parameters include start glucose slider, insulin given slider, and food type selection. Shows in real time how taking accurate bolus insulin changes the curve outcome.

Lab input configuration panel: start glucose set to 88 mg/dL, insulin given at 0 units, meal carbs at 18g with Sushi selected as the food type (different foods have different glycemic index profiles affecting the curve shape). Personal Setup section allows entering individual Carb Ratio (ICR) and Correction Factor (CF) for personalized medical calculations. Scout Swap Strategy toggle applies community meal hacks.

IOB Math — Insulin Decay Engine

Active Insulin / Metabolic Decay Engine: calculates insulin on board (IOB) using a standardized clinical quadratic decay model. Shown here: 10 units injected 60 minutes ago = 5.63 units still active (56% remaining). The 4-hour action curve plots the full decay from bolus to tail. Sliders allow any injection size and time since injection. Built for diabetics who need to know how much active insulin they have before eating again — a critical safety calculation.

Supply Finder & Community

Supply Finder: a categorized directory of free and low-cost insulin and diabetes supply resources for uninsured or underinsured diabetics. Filterable by Emergency/Mutual Aid, Non-Profit Assistance, Manufacturer Program, Community Hub, and Directory. Entries include Mutual Aid Diabetes, Insulin for Life USA, The Embrace Foundation, Lilly Cares Foundation, NovoCare, and Sanofi Patient Connection — with descriptions, tags, and direct links.

Community Events section: diabetes camps, clinics, and meetups for Type 1 and Type 2 diabetics to connect. Listings include BearSkin Meadow Camp (Diabetes Youth Families, Sequoia National Forest) and UCSF Benioff Pediatric Diabetes Clinic Events, each with location tags, category hashtags, and direct website links. Tip banner highlights early registration deadlines.

Tips & Feature Overview

Diabetic Tips section: 5 structured dining strategies in a grid card layout — Check Menus Online First, Beware of Hidden Sugars, Protein & Fiber First, Watch the Beverages, and Modifications are Your Friend. Each card provides specific, actionable guidance rather than generic advice. The full nav shows all 9 live sections of the application.

Restaurant Guide

Searchable database of chains with Safe Bet, The Trap, and Pro Tip for each — filterable by Casual Dining, Fast Food, Coffee & Juice, Sweets & Bakeries.

Sauce Scanner

Interactive sugar meter + full condiment database rated Deadly/Danger/Caution/Safe with exact sugar and carb counts per serving.

The Lab

Glucose curve simulator with food type selector, personal ICR/CF inputs, and real-time curve output showing estimated blood glucose over 4 hours.

IOB Math

Insulin on board calculator using clinical quadratic decay model — shows active insulin remaining at any time point after injection.

Supply Finder

Free resource directory for uninsured diabetics — mutual aid networks, manufacturer programs, non-profits, and community hubs.

Community & Events

Camps, clinics, and meetups for T1D and T2D communities including UCSF Benioff clinic listings and Bay Area programs.

04

Software · C++ · Systems Programming

ASCII First Person Shooter

Built a first-person shooter rendered entirely in ASCII characters through the Windows command prompt. Implemented screen buffers, raycasting-style map rendering, time differentials, directional key movement, and collision detection — all in raw C++. No graphics library, no engine — just the terminal and math.

C++Screen BuffersRaycastingCollision DetectionTime Differential
02

Full-Stack Web · Real-Time · Supabase · React — Spring 2026

Campus Board

I built Campus Board because I kept seeing the same problem repeat itself at SJSU — students needing something last minute with no easy way to reach each other. A calculator before an exam. Leftover food after a club meeting. A charger in the library. There was no good place to ask. So I built one. Campus Board is a real-time community board exclusively for SJSU students, where you can post giveaways, borrowable items, food, study groups, events, rides, and more — all locked behind SJSU email verification so it stays within the campus community.

ReactViteSupabasePostgreSQLRow-Level SecurityRealtime SubscriptionsResend SMTPVercelpg_cronMagic Link Auth
Live App ↗

The Feed — Where Everything Lives

The main feed is the heartbeat of the app. Posts update in real time using Supabase realtime subscriptions — no refreshing needed. Every post has an expiry window (1h, 3h, 6h, or 24h) so the feed stays current and relevant. You can filter by category or search by keyword, and the whole thing is locked to verified SJSU emails only so it stays within the community.

Creating a Post — Intentionally Simple

I wanted posting to feel like it takes ten seconds, not ten minutes. Pick a category, fill out three fields, choose how long it stays up, and hit post. The form adapts its placeholder text based on what you're creating so people actually know what to write. For borrow posts, an amber warning banner reminds both sides that a student ID exchange is required before anything changes hands — that accountability layer is baked right into the interface, not hidden in a terms page nobody reads.

My Activity — What Happens After Someone Claims

The hardest design problem was figuring out what happens after a claim. Just marking something "taken" isn't enough — the two people still need to actually connect. I built a My Activity tab that handles this. Under My Claims, you see the poster's verified SJSU email with a pre-filled email button that opens your mail client with the subject line already written. You can also cancel a claim to release a post back to the feed, or the original poster can mark it resolved once the exchange is done.

What's Under the Hood

SJSU-Only Auth

Signup validates @sjsu.edu on the client, then Supabase magic link auth handles the rest. No passwords, no friction — just a verification email and you're in.

Row-Level Security

Every database policy is enforced with RLS. You can only modify your own posts, claims check that you're not the original poster, and nothing leaks between users.

Auto-Expiry via pg_cron

A Supabase background cron job runs hourly and marks expired posts at the database level. The feed stays clean without any client-side workarounds.

Custom SMTP via Resend

Supabase's default 3 emails/hour would make a campus app unusable. I wired in Resend as a custom SMTP provider to permanently lift that limit for production.

Real-Time Feed

Supabase Realtime pushes database changes to every connected client instantly. Post something and it shows up for everyone on campus — no refresh, no delay.

Deep Link Sharing

Every post generates a shareable URL with its ID. Opening one highlights that specific post with a pulsing border so it's immediately visible in the feed.

Why I built this

I've been that student hunting for a calculator 10 minutes before an exam. I've watched clubs throw away a full tray of food because there was no fast way to tell anyone it was available. Campus Board is my attempt to fix that — not with a complicated platform, but with the simplest thing that actually works. Real students, real needs, real time.
